trajectory_summary: The rep inherited the warmest possible opening — a customer who volunteered his own legitimacy context and was genuinely impressed — and converted it into a disengaged website-deferral in under two and a half minutes. The call never entered the verify sequence, never anchored a bill, never created curiosity, and ended with a soft callback promise that has no locked time. Energy leaked from the first pitch line and never recovered.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the observable outcome is a non-appointment: the rep agreed to send a website link and the customer said 'I'll call you back' with no date, time, or commitment. Per analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, this is a callback WITHOUT a locked time — materially different from a booked sit. If v1 tagged this as any form of appointment or callback, that tag should be reclassified as lost/deferred with no commitment.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: For solar panels for your home, we're offering the bill swap program. We look at your power bill. We actually lower it and replace it for, we would lower it and replace it with a cheaper solar bill.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Every downstream failure in this call — the cost-transparency objection chain, the 'you don't know what I'm paying' rebuttal, the website exit — traces back to this single structural error. The rep pitched before qualifying, which meant every claim he made was ungrounded and every customer question exposed that gap. The warm open gave Bucky a clear runway to run verify as a conversation; he used it to pitch instead. Until this sequencing is fixed, the rep will keep generating the same objection chain on warm leads.

key_moments.quote: Hey. This is Bucky with Ion. Do you know, Piet?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep's intro is a name-check question, not an introduction. The standard intro template requires a company introduction, a form-submission reference, and a human pause for response. The customer supplied all of that himself at 00:15 — but the rep never established legitimacy, never referenced the form, and never delivered the intro beat. The customer's warm response rescued the opening; the rep did not earn it.

key_moments.quote: For solar panels for your home, we're offering the bill swap program. We look at your power bill. We actually lower it and replace it for, we would lower it and replace it with a cheaper solar bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep skipped the entire verify sequence and went straight to a pitch. The customer was warm and had already volunteered the form-fill context — this was the ideal moment to run verify as a friendly conversation. Instead the rep pitched features, which immediately triggered the cost-transparency objection chain. This is a structural sequencing failure, not a persuasion failure.

key_moments.quote: Well, what are the back end costs? If there's no front up upfront, what's the back end?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the bill anchor moment the rep never set up. The customer is asking about cost structure because the rep made a 'no upfront cost' claim without knowing the customer's bill. Had the rep run verify and captured the bill first, this question becomes the setup for the flip — 'what you're paying Dominion right now is the back-end cost we're replacing.' Instead the rep is defending a claim he can't ground.

key_moments.quote: You do realize they're putting data centers in. Right? And that that's hiking everybody's bills?
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer is doing the rep's emotional alignment work. He's not just asking about costs — he's volunteering a reason why his bill is going up and why solar is timely. This is an enthusiasm signal: the customer is already sold on the problem. The rep's response ('Yeah. But that's why the bill swap program is so cool') acknowledges the point but immediately retreats into feature reassurance instead of anchoring on the customer's own framing.
